methods.name: arch.univariate.base.ARCHModel.forecast
documentation:
Construct forecasts from estimated model

# Parameters

params

: Parameters required to forecast. Must be identical in
  shape to the parameters computed by fitting the model.

horizon

: Number of steps to forecast

start

: An integer, datetime or str indicating the first observation to
  produce the forecast for. Datetimes can only be used with pandas
  inputs that have a datetime index. Strings must be convertible
  to a date time, such as in '1945-01-01'.

align

: Either 'origin' or 'target'. When set of 'origin', the t-th row
  of forecasts contains the forecasts for t+1, t+2, ..., t+h. When
  set to 'target', the t-th row contains the 1-step ahead forecast
  from time t-1, the 2 step from time t-2, ..., and the h-step from
  time t-h. 'target' simplified computing forecast errors since the
  realization and h-step forecast are aligned.

method

: Method to use when producing the forecast. The default is analytic.
  The method only affects the variance forecast generation. Not all
  volatility models support all methods. In particular, volatility
  models that do not evolve in squares such as EGARCH or TARCH do not
  support the 'analytic' method for horizons > 1.

simulations

: Number of simulations to run when computing the forecast using
  either simulation or bootstrap.

rng

: Custom random number generator to use in simulation-based forecasts.
  Must produce random samples using the syntax `rng(size)` where size
  the 2-element tuple (simulations, horizon).

random_state

: NumPy RandomState instance to use when method is 'bootstrap'

reindex

: Whether to reindex the forecasts to have the same dimension as the series
  being forecast. Prior to 4.18 this was the default. As of 4.19 this is
  now optional. If not provided, a warning is raised about the future
  change in the default which will occur after September 2021.

  :::{versionadded} 4.19
  :::

x

: Values to use for exogenous regressors if any are included in the
  model. Three formats are accepted:

  - 2-d array-like: This format can be used when there is a single
    exogenous variable. The input must have shape (nforecast, horizon)
    or (nobs, horizon) where nforecast is the number of forecasting
    periods and nobs is the original shape of y. For example, if a
    single series of forecasts are made from the end of the sample
    with a horizon of 10, then the input can be (1, 10). Alternatively,
    if the original data had 1000 observations, then the input can be
    (1000, 10), and only the final row is used to produce forecasts.
    When using an (nobs, horizon) array, the values much be aligned
    so that all values in row t are all out-of-sample at time-t.
  - A dictionary of 2-d array-like: This format is identical to the
    previous except that the dictionary keys must match the names of
    the exog variables. Requires that the exog variables were
    passed as a pandas DataFrame.
  - A 3-d NumPy array (or equivalent). In this format, each panel
    (0th axis) is a 2-d array that must have shape (nforecast, horizon)
    or (nobs,horizon). The array x[j] corresponds to the j-th column of
    the exogenous variables.

  Due to the complexity required to accommodate all scenarios, please
  see the example notebook that demonstrates the valid formats for
  x, and discusses alignment.

  :::{versionadded} 4.19
  :::

# Returns

arch.univariate.base.ARCHModelForecast

: Container for forecasts. Key properties are `mean`,
  `variance` and `residual_variance`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import pandas as pd
>>> from arch import arch_model
>>> am = arch_model(None,mean='HAR',lags=[1,5,22],vol='Constant')
>>> sim_data = am.simulate([0.1,0.4,0.3,0.2,1.0], 250)
>>> sim_data.index = pd.date_range('2000-01-01',periods=250)
>>> am = arch_model(sim_data['data'],mean='HAR',lags=[1,5,22],  vol='Constant')
>>> res = am.fit()
>>> fig = res.hedgehog_plot()
```

# Notes

The most basic 1-step ahead forecast will return a vector with the same
length as the original data, where the t-th value will be the time-t
forecast for time t + 1. When the horizon is > 1, and when using the
default value for `align`, the forecast value in position [t, h] is the
time-t, h+1 step ahead forecast.

If model contains exogenous variables (model.x is not None), then
only 1-step ahead forecasts are available. Using horizon > 1 will
produce a warning and all columns, except the first, will be
nan-filled.

If `align` is 'origin', forecast[t,h] contains the forecast made using
y[:t] (that is, up to but not including t) for horizon h + 1. For
example, y[100,2] contains the 3-step ahead forecast using the first
100 data points, which will correspond to the realization y[100 + 2].
If `align` is 'target', then the same forecast is in location
[102, 2], so that it is aligned with the observation to use when
evaluating, but still in the same column.

Abstract base class for mean models in ARCH processes.  Specifies the
conditional mean process.

All public methods that raise NotImplementedError should be overridden by
any subclass.  Private methods that raise NotImplementedError are optional
to override but recommended where applicable.

If model contains exogenous variables (`model.x is not None`), then
only 1-step ahead forecasts are available. Using horizon > 1 will
produce a warning and all columns, except the first, will be
nan-filled.

If `align` is 'origin', forecast[t,h] contains the forecast made using
y[:t] (that is, up to but not including t) for horizon h + 1. For
example, y[100,2] contains the 3-step ahead forecast using the first
100 data points, which will correspond to the realization y[100 + 2].
If `align` is 'target', then the same forecast is in location
[102, 2], so that it is aligned with the observation to use when
evaluating, but still in the same column.

methods.name: arch.univariate.base.ARCHModelFixedResult.hedgehog_plot
documentation:
Plot forecasts from estimated model

# Parameters

params

: Alternative parameters to use. If not provided, the parameters
  computed by fitting the model are used. Must be 1-d and identical
  in shape to the parameters computed by fitting the model.

horizon

: Number of steps to forecast

step

: Non-negative number of forecasts to skip between spines

start

: An integer, datetime or str indicating the first observation to
  produce the forecast for. Datetimes can only be used with pandas
  inputs that have a datetime index. Strings must be convertible
  to a date time, such as in '1945-01-01'. If not provided, the start
  is set to the earliest forecastable date.

plot_type

: Quantity to plot, the forecast volatility or the forecast mean

method

: Method to use when producing the forecast. The default is analytic.
  The method only affects the variance forecast generation. Not all
  volatility models support all methods. In particular, volatility
  models that do not evolve in squares such as EGARCH or TARCH do not
  support the 'analytic' method for horizons > 1.

simulations

: Number of simulations to run when computing the forecast using
  either simulation or bootstrap.

# Returns

fig

: Handle to the figure

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import pandas as pd
>>> from arch import arch_model
>>> am = arch_model(None,mean='HAR',lags=[1,5,22],vol='Constant')
>>> sim_data = am.simulate([0.1,0.4,0.3,0.2,1.0], 250)
>>> sim_data.index = pd.date_range('2000-01-01',periods=250)
>>> am = arch_model(sim_data['data'],mean='HAR',lags=[1,5,22],  vol='Constant')
>>> res = am.fit()
>>> fig = res.hedgehog_plot(plot_type='mean')
```

methods.name: arch.univariate.distribution.Normal.loglikelihood
documentation:
Computes the log-likelihood of assuming residuals are normally
distributed, conditional on the variance

# Parameters

parameters

: The normal likelihood has no shape parameters. Empty since the
  standard normal has no shape parameters.

resids

: The residuals to use in the log-likelihood calculation

sigma2

: Conditional variances of resids

individual

: Flag indicating whether to return the vector of individual log
  likelihoods (True) or the sum (False)

# Returns

ll

: The log-likelihood

# Notes

The log-likelihood of a single data point x is

$$
\ln f\left(x\right)=-\frac{1}{2}\left(\ln2\pi+\ln\sigma^{2}
+\frac{x^{2}}{\sigma^{2}}\right)
$$

classes.name: arch.univariate.distribution.StudentsT

methods.name: arch.univariate.distribution.StudentsT.loglikelihood
documentation:
Computes the log-likelihood of assuming residuals are have a
standardized (to have unit variance) Student's t distribution,
conditional on the variance.

# Parameters

parameters

: Shape parameter of the t distribution

resids

: The residuals to use in the log-likelihood calculation

sigma2

: Conditional variances of resids

individual

: Flag indicating whether to return the vector of individual log
  likelihoods (True) or the sum (False)

# Returns

ll

: The log-likelihood

# Notes

The log-likelihood of a single data point x is

$$
\ln\Gamma\left(\frac{\nu+1}{2}\right)
-\ln\Gamma\left(\frac{\nu}{2}\right)
-\frac{1}{2}\ln(\pi\left(\nu-2\right)\sigma^{2})
-\frac{\nu+1}{2}\ln(1+x^{2}/(\sigma^{2}(\nu-2)))
$$

where $\Gamma$ is the gamma function.

classes.name: arch.univariate.distribution.SkewStudent

methods.name: arch.univariate.distribution.SkewStudent.loglikelihood
documentation:
Computes the log-likelihood of assuming residuals are have a
standardized (to have unit variance) Skew Student's t distribution,
conditional on the variance.

# Parameters

parameters

: Shape parameter of the skew-t distribution

resids

: The residuals to use in the log-likelihood calculation

sigma2

: Conditional variances of resids

individual

: Flag indicating whether to return the vector of individual log
  likelihoods (True) or the sum (False)

# Returns

ll

: The log-likelihood

# Notes

The log-likelihood of a single data point x is

$$
\ln\left[\frac{bc}{\sigma}\left(1+\frac{1}{\eta-2}
    \left(\frac{a+bx/\sigma}
    {1+sgn(x/\sigma+a/b)\lambda}\right)^{2}\right)
    ^{-\left(\eta+1\right)/2}\right],
$$

where $2<\eta<\infty$, and $-1<\lambda<1$.
The constants $a$, $b$, and $c$ are given by

$$
a=4\lambda c\frac{\eta-2}{\eta-1},
    \quad b^{2}=1+3\lambda^{2}-a^{2},
    \quad c=\frac{\Gamma\left(\frac{\eta+1}{2}\right)}
    {\sqrt{\pi\left(\eta-2\right)}
    \Gamma\left(\frac{\eta}{2}\right)},
$$

and $\Gamma$ is the gamma function.

Standardized Skewed Student's distribution for use with ARCH models

# Parameters

random_state
: :::{deprecated} 5.0
  random_state is deprecated. Use seed instead.
  :::

seed

: Random number generator instance or int to use. Set to ensure
  reproducibility. If using an int, the argument is passed to
  `np.random.default_rng`. If not provided, `default_rng`
  is used with system-provided entropy.

# Notes

The Standardized Skewed Student's distribution ([^footnote-1]) takes two parameters,
$\eta$ and $\lambda$. $\eta$ controls the tail shape
and is similar to the shape parameter in a Standardized Student's t.
$\lambda$ controls the skewness. When $\lambda=0$ the
distribution is identical to a standardized Student's t.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Hansen, B. E. (1994). Autoregressive conditional density estimation.
    *International Economic Review*, 35(3), 705–730.
    \<<https://www.ssc.wisc.edu/~bhansen/papers/ier_94.pdf>>

classes.name: arch.univariate.distribution.GeneralizedError

methods.name: arch.univariate.distribution.GeneralizedError.loglikelihood
documentation:
Computes the log-likelihood of assuming residuals are have a
Generalized Error Distribution, conditional on the variance.

# Parameters

parameters

: Shape parameter of the GED distribution

resids

: The residuals to use in the log-likelihood calculation

sigma2

: Conditional variances of resids

individual

: Flag indicating whether to return the vector of individual log
  likelihoods (True) or the sum (False)

# Returns

ll

: The log-likelihood

# Notes

The log-likelihood of a single data point x is

$$
\ln\nu-\ln c-\ln\Gamma(\frac{1}{\nu})-(1+\frac{1}{\nu})\ln2
-\frac{1}{2}\ln\sigma^{2}
-\frac{1}{2}\left|\frac{x}{c\sigma}\right|^{\nu}
$$

where $\Gamma$ is the gamma function and $\ln c$ is

$$
\ln c=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{-2}{\nu}\ln2+\ln\Gamma(\frac{1}{\nu})
-\ln\Gamma(\frac{3}{\nu})\right).
$$

methods.name: arch.univariate.volatility.VolatilityProcess.forecast
documentation:
Forecast volatility from the model

# Parameters

parameters

: Parameters required to forecast the volatility model

resids

: Residuals to use in the recursion

backcast

: Value to use when initializing the recursion

var_bounds

: Array containing columns of lower and upper bounds

start

: Index of the first observation to use as the starting point for
  the forecast. Default is len(resids).

horizon

: Forecast horizon. Must be 1 or larger. Forecasts are produced
  for horizons in [1, horizon].

method

: Method to use when producing the forecast. The default is analytic.

simulations

: Number of simulations to run when computing the forecast using
  either simulation or bootstrap.

rng

: Callable random number generator required if method is
  'simulation'. Must take a single shape input and return random
  samples numbers with that shape.

random_state

: NumPy RandomState instance to use when method is 'bootstrap'

# Returns

forecasts

: Class containing the variance forecasts, and, if using simulation
  or bootstrap, the simulated paths.

# Raises

NotImplementedError
: - If method is not supported

ValueError
: - If the method is not known

# Notes

The analytic `method` is not supported for all models. Attempting
to use this method when not available will raise a ValueError.

classes.name: arch.univariate.volatility.GARCH
documentation:
GARCH and related model estimation

The following models can be specified using GARCH:
: - ARCH(p)
  - GARCH(p,q)
  - GJR-GARCH(p,o,q)
  - AVARCH(p)
  - AVGARCH(p,q)
  - TARCH(p,o,q)
  - Models with arbitrary, pre-specified powers

# Parameters

p

: Order of the symmetric innovation

o

: Order of the asymmetric innovation

q

: Order of the lagged (transformed) conditional variance

power

: Power to use with the innovations, abs(e) \*\* power. Default is 2.0, which
  produces ARCH and related models. Using 1.0 produces AVARCH and related
  models. Other powers can be specified, although these should be strictly
  positive, and usually larger than 0.25.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from arch.univariate import GARCH
```

Standard GARCH(1,1)

```pycon
>>> garch = GARCH(p=1, q=1)
```

Asymmetric GJR-GARCH process

```pycon
>>> gjr = GARCH(p=1, o=1, q=1)
```

Asymmetric TARCH process

```pycon
>>> tarch = GARCH(p=1, o=1, q=1, power=1.0)
```

# Notes

In this class of processes, the variance dynamics are

$$
\sigma_{t}^{\lambda}=\omega
+ \sum_{i=1}^{p}\alpha_{i}\left|\epsilon_{t-i}\right|^{\lambda}
+\sum_{j=1}^{o}\gamma_{j}\left|\epsilon_{t-j}\right|^{\lambda}
I\left[\epsilon_{t-j}<0\right]+\sum_{k=1}^{q}\beta_{k}\sigma_{t-k}^{\lambda}
$$

where $\lambda$ is the `power`.

classes.name: arch.univariate.volatility.HARCH
documentation:
Heterogeneous ARCH process

# Parameters

lags

: List of lags to include in the model, or if scalar, includes all lags up the
  value

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from arch.univariate import HARCH
```

Lag-1 HARCH, which is identical to an ARCH(1)

```pycon
>>> harch = HARCH()
```

More useful and realistic lag lengths

```pycon
>>> harch = HARCH(lags=[1, 5, 22])
```

# Notes

In a Heterogeneous ARCH process, variance dynamics are

$$
\sigma_{t}^{2}=\omega + \sum_{i=1}^{m}\alpha_{l_{i}}
\left(l_{i}^{-1}\sum_{j=1}^{l_{i}}\epsilon_{t-j}^{2}\right)
$$

In the common case where `lags=[1,5,22]`, the model is

$$
\sigma_{t}^{2}=\omega+\alpha_{1}\epsilon_{t-1}^{2}
+\alpha_{5} \left(\frac{1}{5}\sum_{j=1}^{5}\epsilon_{t-j}^{2}\right)
+\alpha_{22} \left(\frac{1}{22}\sum_{j=1}^{22}\epsilon_{t-j}^{2}\right)
$$

A HARCH process is a special case of an ARCH process where parameters in the
more general ARCH process have been restricted.

classes.name: arch.univariate.volatility.MIDASHyperbolic

methods.name: arch.univariate.volatility.MIDASHyperbolic.constraints
documentation:
Constraints

Notes
-----
Parameters are (omega, alpha, gamma, theta)

A.dot(parameters) - b >= 0

1. omega >0
2. alpha>0 or alpha + gamma > 0
3. alpha<1 or alpha+0.5*gamma<1
4. theta > 0
5. theta < 1

MIDAS Hyperbolic ARCH process

# Parameters

m

: Length of maximum lag to include in the model

asym

: Flag indicating whether to include an asymmetric term

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from arch.univariate import MIDASHyperbolic
```

22-lag MIDAS Hyperbolic process

```pycon
>>> harch = MIDASHyperbolic()
```

Longer 66-period lag

```pycon
>>> harch = MIDASHyperbolic(m=66)
```

Asymmetric MIDAS Hyperbolic process

```pycon
>>> harch = MIDASHyperbolic(asym=True)
```

# Notes

In a MIDAS Hyperbolic process, the variance evolves according to

$$
\sigma_{t}^{2}=\omega+
\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\alpha+\gamma I\left[\epsilon_{t-j}<0\right]\right)
\phi_{i}(\theta)\epsilon_{t-i}^{2}
$$

where

$$
\phi_{i}(\theta) \propto \Gamma(i+\theta)/(\Gamma(i+1)\Gamma(\theta))
$$

where $\Gamma$ is the gamma function. $\{\phi_i(\theta)\}$ is
normalized so that $\sum \phi_i(\theta)=1$. See [^footnote-1] and [^footnote-2] for
further details.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Foroni, Claudia, and Massimiliano Marcellino. "A survey of
    Econometric Methods for Mixed-Frequency Data". Norges Bank. (2013).

[^footnote-2]: Sheppard, Kevin. "Direct volatility modeling". Manuscript. (2018).

ARCH process

p

# Examples

ARCH(1) process

```pycon
>>> from arch.univariate import ARCH
```

ARCH(5) process


classes.name: arch.univariate.volatility.EWMAVariance
documentation:
Exponentially Weighted Moving-Average (RiskMetrics) Variance process

# Parameters

lam

: Smoothing parameter. Default is 0.94. Set to None to estimate lam
  jointly with other model parameters

# Examples

Daily RiskMetrics EWMA process

```pycon
>>> from arch.univariate import EWMAVariance
>>> rm = EWMAVariance(0.94)
```

# Notes

The variance dynamics of the model

$$
\sigma_t^{2}=\lambda\sigma_{t-1}^2 + (1-\lambda)\epsilon^2_{t-1}
$$

When lam is provided, this model has no parameters since the smoothing
parameter is treated as fixed. Set lam to `None` to jointly estimate this
parameter when fitting the model.

Construct values for backcasting to start the recursion

Parameters

    Vector of (approximate) residuals

-------
backcast : ndarray
    Backcast values for each EWMA component

RiskMetrics 2006 Variance process

# Parameters

tau0

: Length of long cycle. Default is 1560.

tau1

: Length of short cycle. Default is 4.

kmax

: Number of components. Default is 14.

rho

: Relative scale of adjacent cycles. Default is sqrt(2)

# Examples

Daily RiskMetrics 2006 process

```pycon
>>> from arch.univariate import RiskMetrics2006
>>> rm = RiskMetrics2006()
```

# Notes

The variance dynamics of the model are given as a weighted average of kmax EWMA
variance processes where the smoothing parameters and weights are determined by
tau0, tau1 and rho.

This model has no parameters since the smoothing parameter is fixed.

classes.name: arch.univariate.volatility.EGARCH
documentation:
EGARCH model estimation

# Parameters

p

: Order of the symmetric innovation

o

: Order of the asymmetric innovation

q

: Order of the lagged (transformed) conditional variance

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from arch.univariate import EGARCH
```

Symmetric EGARCH(1,1)

```pycon
>>> egarch = EGARCH(p=1, q=1)
```

Standard EGARCH process

```pycon
>>> egarch = EGARCH(p=1, o=1, q=1)
```

Exponential ARCH process

```pycon
>>> earch = EGARCH(p=5)
```

# Notes

In this class of processes, the variance dynamics are

$$
\ln\sigma_{t}^{2}=\omega
+\sum_{i=1}^{p}\alpha_{i}
\left(\left|e_{t-i}\right|-\sqrt{2/\pi}\right)
+\sum_{j=1}^{o}\gamma_{j} e_{t-j}
+\sum_{k=1}^{q}\beta_{k}\ln\sigma_{t-k}^{2}
$$

where $e_{t}=\epsilon_{t}/\sigma_{t}$.

classes.name: arch.univariate.volatility.FixedVariance
documentation:
Fixed volatility process

# Parameters

variance

: Array containing the variances to use. Should have the same shape as the
  data used in the model. This is not checked since the model is not
  available when the FixedVariance process is created.

unit_scale

: Flag whether to enforce a unit scale. If False, a scale parameter will be
  estimated so that the model variance will be proportional to `variance`.
  If True, the model variance is set of `variance`

# Notes

Allows a fixed set of variances to be used when estimating a mean model,
allowing GLS estimation.

classes.name: arch.univariate.volatility.FIGARCH

methods.name: arch.univariate.volatility.FIGARCH.truncation
documentation: Truncation lag for the ARCH-infinity approximation

FIGARCH model

# Parameters

p

: Order of the symmetric innovation

q

: Order of the lagged (transformed) conditional variance

power

: Power to use with the innovations, abs(e) \*\* power. Default is 2.0,
  which produces FIGARCH and related models. Using 1.0 produces
  FIAVARCH and related models. Other powers can be specified, although
  these should be strictly positive, and usually larger than 0.25.

truncation

: Truncation point to use in ARCH($\infty$) representation.
  Default is 1000.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from arch.univariate import FIGARCH
```

Standard FIGARCH

```pycon
>>> figarch = FIGARCH()
```

FIARCH

```pycon
>>> fiarch = FIGARCH(p=0)
```

FIAVGARCH process

```pycon
>>> fiavarch = FIGARCH(power=1.0)
```

# Notes

In this class of processes, the variance dynamics are

$$
h_t = \omega + [1-\beta L - \phi L  (1-L)^d] \epsilon_t^2 + \beta h_{t-1}
$$

where `L` is the lag operator and `d` is the fractional differencing
parameter. The model is estimated using the ARCH($\infty$)
representation,

$$
h_t = (1-\beta)^{-1}  \omega + \sum_{i=1}^\infty \lambda_i \epsilon_{t-i}^2
$$

The weights are constructed using

$$
\delta_1 = d \\
\lambda_1 = d - \beta + \phi
$$

and the recursive equations

$$
\delta_j = \frac{j - 1 - d}{j}  \delta_{j-1} \\
\lambda_j = \beta \lambda_{j-1} + \delta_j - \phi \delta_{j-1}.
$$

When `power` is not 2, the ARCH($\infty$) representation is still used
where $\epsilon_t^2$ is replaced by $|\epsilon_t|^p$ and
`p` is the power.

classes.name: arch.univariate.volatility.APARCH

methods.name: arch.univariate.volatility.APARCH.delta
documentation: The value of delta in the model. NaN is delta is estimated.

methods.name: arch.univariate.volatility.APARCH.common_asym
documentation: The value of delta in the model. NaN is delta is estimated.

Asymmetric Power ARCH (APARCH) volatility process

# Parameters

p

: Order of the symmetric innovation. Must satisfy p>=o.

o

: Order of the asymmetric innovation. Must satisfy o\<=p.

q

: Order of the lagged (transformed) conditional variance

delta

: Value to use for a fixed delta in the APARCH model. If
  not provided, the value of delta is jointly estimated
  with other model parameters. User provided delta is restricted
  to lie in (0.05, 4.0).

common_asym

: Restrict all asymmetry terms to share the same asymmetry
  parameter. If False (default), then there are no restrictions
  on the `o` asymmetry parameters.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from arch.univariate import APARCH
```

Symmetric Power ARCH(1,1)

```pycon
>>> aparch = APARCH(p=1, q=1)
```

Standard APARCH process

```pycon
>>> aparch = APARCH(p=1, o=1, q=1)
```

Fixed power parameters

```pycon
>>> aparch = APARCH(p=1, o=1, q=1, delta=1.3)
```

# Notes

In this class of processes, the variance dynamics are

$$
\sigma_{t}^{\delta}=\omega
+\sum_{i=1}^{p}\alpha_{i}
\left(\left|\epsilon_{t-i}\right|
-\gamma_{i}I_{[o\geq i]}\epsilon_{t-i}\right)^{\delta}
+\sum_{k=1}^{q}\beta_{k}\sigma_{t-k}^{\delta}
$$

If `common_asym` is `True`, then all of $\gamma_i$
are restricted to have a common value.
